mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers Archaic and Classical Greek civilization including the poleis, Sparta, Athens, Philip II, and Alexander, the core of subsections B and C.
Covers the Roman Republic from its founding through the Punic Wars, Gracchi reforms, and Caesar's dictatorship, directly relevant to subsections D3-D5.
Covers the Roman Empire from Augustus through the 4th century including provincial administration and decline, directly relevant to subsection E.
Directly covers subsection B2h on Alexander's empire, his conquest of Persia, and the ideals and governing practices of his rule.
Covers the Hellenistic kingdoms after Alexander including the Ptolemaic and Seleucid states, directly relevant to subsection C.
Directly covers subsection A3a on Etruscan origins, language, cities, government, art, and their formative influence over early Rome.
Directly relevant to subsection B2c on the Peloponnesian War and Thucydides' account of the conflict.
Directly relevant to subsection B1c-viii on the Greco-Persian Wars and Herodotus' account of the conflict.
Relevant to subsection B1b on the world of Homer and Hesiod in the Geometric Period of early Greek civilization.
Covers Greek and Latin literary traditions including drama, epic, and lyric poetry relevant to subsections B2a-iii, B2g, and E1d on cultural developments.
Directly covers subsection A3b-i on Celtic occupation of Europe, their penetration into Italy and the Balkans, and their art and social organization.
Directly relevant to subsection B1c-ii on Spartan dominance over the Peloponnese and B2c-d on Spartan hegemony after the Peloponnesian War.
Covers Greek and Roman military practices relevant to the extensive warfare discussed across subsections B, C, D, and E.
Relevant to subsections B1c-vii and C3b on the beginnings of philosophy, science, and Hellenistic scientific developments.
Covers the transformation of Greco-Roman culture and the rise of Christianity in the 3rd-4th centuries, directly relevant to subsection E4.
Covers the founding of Constantinople and the transition from Roman to Byzantine Empire, relevant to subsection E4e-ii.
Relevant to subsection E4b-c on the rise and spread of Christianity in the Roman Empire and its establishment as state religion.
Directly relevant to subsection E1d on the culture of the Augustan Age and the contributions of Horace.
Directly relevant to subsection E1d on the culture of the Augustan Age and the contributions of Ovid.
Directly relevant to subsection D5h on oratory and philosophy in the late Republic, where Cicero was a central figure.
Directly relevant to subsection B2c on intellectual changes at Athens during the Peloponnesian War, specifically the Sophists and Socrates.
Relevant to subsection B2g on developments in philosophy in 4th-century Greece.
Provides a broad introduction to the study of Classical Greek and Roman civilization, relevant to the section's overall scope.
Covers Graeco-Roman philosophical traditions relevant to subsections B1c-vii, B2g, and E3b on intellectual developments.
Relevant to subsection C3b and E3b on Hellenistic and Greco-Roman philosophical developments including Stoicism.
Relevant to subsection C3b on Hellenistic developments in philosophy.
Relevant to subsection E4f on the remnants of pagan culture and Julian's attempt to restore the old religion.
Relevant to subsection A3b-i on Celtic religion and social organization in trans-Alpine Europe.
Directly relevant to subsection E1e on the annexation of Britain under Claudius and the provinces under the later empire.
Relevant to subsection B1c-ii on the institution of democracy at Athens under Cleisthenes and its further development under Pericles.
Relevant to subsection B2a-iii on cultural efflorescence in Periclean Athens, including drama as a major art form.
Relevant to Homer and Hesiod in the Geometric Period and Virgil in the Augustan Age, both central to the epic literary tradition.
Relevant to Greek mythology, religious developments, and panhellenic centres like Olympia and Delphi discussed in subsection B1b.
Relevant to subsection D5h on oratory and philosophy in the late Roman Republic.
